perturbation.original_text: The Recipient of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall promptly notify the disclosing Party of any disclosure of such Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement or of any subpoena or other legal process requiring production or disclosure of said Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall notify the disclosing Party of any disclosure of such Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the disclosing party of a subpoena or other legal process requiring disclosure introduces legal risk. Florida law requires compliance with subpoenas, and hindering notification could lead to legal complications.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Florida Statute 918.13, which addresses obstruction of justice. By removing the obligation to inform the disclosing party about legal processes like subpoenas, the contract potentially hinders their ability to legally challenge the disclosure, which could be seen as obstructing the administration of justice.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XLVII > Chapter 918 > Section 13 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XLVII CRIMINAL PROCEDURE AND CORRECTIONS Chapter 918 CONDUCT OF TRIAL Entire Chapter SECTION 13 Tampering with or fabricating physical evidence. F.S. 918.13 918.13 Tampering with or fabricating physical evidence. — (1) No person, knowing that a criminal trial or proceeding or an investigation by a duly constituted prosecuting authority, law enforcement agency, grand jury or legislative committee of this state is pending or is about to be instituted, shall: (a) Alter, destroy, conceal, or remove any record, document, or thing with the purpose to impair its verity or availability in such proceeding or investigation; or (b) Make, present, or use any record, document, or thing, knowing it to be false. (2) Any person who violates any provision of this section shall be guilty of a felony of the third degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082 , s. 775.083 , or s. 775.084 . History. — s. 2, ch. 72-315.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a key requirement from the original contract: the notification to the disclosing party regarding subpoenas or legal processes demanding the disclosure of confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this removal might hinder the disclosing party's ability to legally challenge the disclosure, potentially obstructing justice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from Florida Statute 918.13, addresses 'Tampering with or fabricating physical evidence' and includes altering, destroying, or concealing records with the purpose of impairing their verity or availability in a proceeding or investigation. While the changed text doesn't directly tamper with evidence, by removing the notification requirement, it creates a situation where the disclosing party is unaware of a legal challenge and cannot take steps to protect the information. This weakening of their ability to act could be construed as hindering the administration of justice. Therefore, the change creates a legal risk that could be interpreted as obstructing justice, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain damages from any court.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'irreparable' and 'injunctive relief' significantly weakens the remedies available to the disclosing party, which conflicts with general principles of contract law that allow for specific performance (injunctive relief) when monetary damages are insufficient, especially concerning trade secrets, where irreparable harm is often presumed.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change undermines the remedies available under the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA). FUTSA explicitly allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets (Fla. Stat. § 688.003). By omitting the reference to injunctive relief, the contract limits the disclosing party's ability to seek immediate cessation of the breach, which is a key component of trade secret protection.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits 'irreparable injury' and 'injunctive relief', weakening the remedies available to the disclosing party. This contradicts the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA), as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states, "(1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By removing the explicit right to seek injunctive relief, the contract limits the disclosing party's ability to prevent ongoing trade secret misappropriation, which is a key protection afforded by FUTSA. The omission directly undermines the statute's provision for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: The requirement to protect Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the survival clause eliminates the protection of confidential information after the agreement's termination, contradicting the intent to keep trade secrets protected and potentially violating Florida law regarding trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 688.002 defines misappropriation of trade secrets, which can occur even after an agreement terminates if the confidential information is used improperly. Removing the survival clause implies that after the agreement ends, the receiving party can freely use the confidential information, directly contradicting the protections intended by FUTSA.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures that the protection of confidential information survives the termination of the agreement. The changed text, which is a complete omission of the original clause, eliminates this protection. Florida Statute § 688.002, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines 'misappropriation' to include the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent' under certain conditions. By removing the survival clause, the changed text arguably allows for the potential misappropriation of trade secrets after the agreement terminates, as there's no contractual obligation preventing the receiving party from using the confidential information. This directly contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets as contemplated by Florida law, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties and in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party or to enter into any other agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'and in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party or to enter into any other agreement', we could imply an obligation to disclose, going against general contract principles requiring explicit agreement. Florida law requires clear and unambiguous contract terms. While subtle, the absence of this disclaimer creates potential ambiguity and a misrepresentation.
perturbation.law_citation: E.g., Boatright v. Universal City Property Owners Ass'n, Inc., 326 So.3d 764 (Fla. 2d DCA 2021)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, Florida law heavily relies on principles of contract interpretation, requiring clear and unambiguous terms. Cases like Boatright v. Universal City Property Owners Ass'n, Inc. (326 So.3d 764 (Fla. 2d DCA 2021)) highlight the importance of explicit agreements. By removing the clause explicitly stating that there's no obligation to disclose or enter into another agreement, the edited contract opens the door to ambiguity and potentially misinterpreting the parties' intentions, in conflict with these established legal principles of contract interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly states that there is no obligation for either party to disclose information or enter into another agreement. The changed text removes this clause, which, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations state, opens the door to ambiguity and potential misinterpretation of the parties' intentions regarding disclosure, conflicting with Florida's principles of contract interpretation that require clear and unambiguous terms. The definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' states that a legal contradiction happens when a contract omits essential information, creating legal loopholes. Here, the omission creates an ambiguity regarding required disclosure, creating a loophole.
